topic: water pump broke — asking about replacement brands

turns.content: water pump break this morning. 7 year old pump, finally die. I need new one fast, friend. what brand good for shrimp pond?

Sorry to hear about the pump — 7 years is a decent run, but never fun when equipment dies on you. What size pump do you need? A few questions:

- How big is the area you're pumping water for?
- Are you pumping water in, out, or circulating?
- What was the old pump's horsepower or flow rate if you remember?

That'll help me point you to the right brands and models.
I use for drain water out from pond and also pump fresh water in from canal. old one is 2 horsepower. Chinese brand, forget name already. it work okay but rust very bad, you know

Okay, 2 HP for drainage and filling — that's a common setup. Rust is the big killer for pumps in pond farming because of the salt and humidity. Here are some solid options:

1. **Honda WB20/WB30** — Japanese, very reliable, easy to find parts in Vietnam. The WB20 is about 2 HP. More expensive upfront but lasts 10+ years with basic maintenance.

2. **Tsurumi** — Japanese brand, built specifically for dirty water and agricultural use. Great anti-corrosion coatings. Popular with fish and shrimp farmers.

3. **Lucky Pro** — Thai brand, good mid-range option. Cheaper than Honda, better build quality than most Chinese pumps. Common in the Mekong area.

4. **Panasonic GP series** — for smaller flow needs, very energy efficient.

For rust resistance, look for **stainless steel impellers** and **cast aluminum or coated housings**. Avoid plain cast iron if you're near brackish water.
Honda I know. my neighbor use Honda pump maybe 12 year already, still run good. but expensive, you know. how much Honda WB20 now?

Honda WB20XT is typically in the range of **5,500,000 to 7,000,000 dong** depending on the dealer and whether it's genuine Honda Vietnam or imported. Some agricultural supply shops in Can Tho or Long Xuyen might have slightly better prices.

The WB30 (3 HP, higher flow) runs about **7,500,000 to 9,000,000 dong**.

Your neighbor's experience is a good sign — 12 years is typical for Honda if you maintain it. The parts are widely available too, so if something goes wrong you're not stuck waiting for special orders.

The Chinese pumps are usually 2,000,000-3,000,000 dong but you're replacing them every 3-5 years and dealing with rust. Over 10 years, Honda actually costs less.
that is what I think too. buy cheap, buy twice. my father always say that, you know. okay Honda WB20 sound good. what about maintenance? what I need to do so it last long?

Your father was a wise man. Here's the maintenance routine for a Honda WB20:

**After every use:**
- Run clean fresh water through it for 2-3 minutes to flush out salt, mud, and debris. This is the single most important thing for longevity.

**Every month:**
- Check and clean the strainer/filter basket
- Inspect the impeller for wear or debris
- Check hose connections for leaks

**Every 3-6 months:**
- Change the engine oil (use Honda GX or equivalent 10W-30)
- Clean or replace the air filter
- Check the spark plug

**Every year:**
- Replace the mechanical seal if you notice any dripping
- Check the impeller for erosion

**Critical tip:** When you store it, never leave water sitting inside. Drain it completely. Stagnant water plus salt equals corrosion, even in a Honda.
flush with clean water after use. that I never do with old pump. maybe that is why it rust so fast, you know. okay I learn from mistake

Exactly — that's probably what killed it. Brackish pond water sitting in the pump housing eats through metal fast. Even 2 minutes of flushing with fresh water makes a huge difference.

Some farmers also spray a light coat of WD-40 or similar on the outside metal parts every couple weeks. Cheap insurance against surface rust.
one more thing friend. right now pump dead, I drain pond by gravity only. very slow. how long I can go without pump before shrimp have problem, you know?

It depends on your current water quality and weather, but generally:

- If your aerators are running and the weather isn't too hot, your shrimp can be okay for **3-5 days** without water exchange.
- If it's very hot (above 33-34°C) and sunny, water heats up fast and oxygen drops — then you might have **1-2 days** before stress shows.
- Watch for shrimp swimming at the surface or along the edges — that's a sign of low oxygen or poor water quality.

So getting to Can Tho tomorrow and setting up the new pump the same day is a good timeline. You should be fine if you keep the aerators going tonight.
okay friend I go to Can Tho tomorrow, look for Honda pump. you think I need buy extra part? like spare impeller or something?

Good idea to pick up a few spares while you're there. I'd grab:

- **1 spare impeller** — this is the part that wears fastest
- **2-3 mechanical seals** — cheap and you'll need them eventually
- **Extra air filter** — easy to forget until it's clogged
- **Spark plug** (1-2 spares)

All together, spare parts should be under 500,000 dong and it'll save you a trip to Can Tho later when something wears out. Ask the dealer if they have a maintenance kit — sometimes they bundle the basics together at a discount.
good advice. I write down: impeller, seal, air filter, spark plug. okay thank you friend. I go early morning, get pump, come back and set up before afternoon. pond cannot wait, you know
Sounds like a solid plan. Get there early and you should be pumping again by afternoon. Hope the Honda serves you well — with proper flushing this time, it should last you a long time. Good luck!
